stem: A support agent handles long multi-turn conversations. Over time, the running summary condenses the order total ($1,249.00), the order date, and the RMA number into vague phrases like "a recent high-value order." The agent then quotes wrong figures. What is the most reliable fix?

options.A: Extract transactional facts (amounts, dates, order/RMA numbers, statuses) into a persistent case facts block included in each prompt, outside the summarized history
options.B: Disable summarization entirely and always pass the full raw history
options.C: Instruct the model to never summarize numeric values
options.D: Increase the model's max_tokens so summaries can be longer

explanation: Progressive summarization reliably degrades precise transactional data (amounts, dates, identifiers). A case facts block stores these values verbatim in a structured block that travels with each prompt OUTSIDE the summarized history, so they are never lost to compression.

distractors.B: Never summarizing eventually overflows the context window in long conversations; the goal is to keep the facts that matter, not all raw text.
distractors.C: A prompt instruction is probabilistic and still degrades over many turns; it does not guarantee the values persist.
distractors.D: Larger max_tokens affects output length, not whether summarization drops precise figures from the running history.

stem: A synthesis agent receives a single long concatenation of findings from five subagents. It consistently captures conclusions from the first and last subagents but omits findings from those in the middle. What is the best mitigation?

options.A: Increase the model's temperature so it explores more of the input
options.B: Truncate the input to only the first and last subagents' findings
options.C: Place a key-findings summary at the beginning and organize the detailed sections with explicit headers
options.D: Process the concatenation in reverse so the middle is seen last

explanation: This is the "lost in the middle" effect: models reliably attend to the beginning and end of long inputs but may skip middle content. Putting a key-findings summary up front and using explicit section headers makes the middle content salient and addressable.

distractors.A: Temperature controls output randomness, not attention distribution across a long input.
distractors.B: Truncating the middle deliberately discards exactly the findings being lost.
distractors.D: Reversing the order just moves the neglected content to a new middle position; the positional bias remains.

stem: A customer's first message is: "Stop wasting my time with bots. I want to speak to a human, now." The requested action (checking an order status) is something the agent can easily do. What should the agent do?

options.A: Acknowledge the frustration, attempt resolution, and escalate only if frustration persists
options.B: Investigate and resolve the order question first, then escalate only if the customer asks again
options.C: Escalate to a human immediately, without first attempting to investigate or resolve
options.D: Ask the customer to explain why they want a human before deciding

explanation: An explicit request for a human is a hard escalation trigger: escalate immediately, no investigation first. The agent should not gate an explicit human request behind a resolution attempt or interrogation.

distractors.A: Offering resolution first is the correct response to implicit frustration alone, not to an explicit demand for a human.
distractors.B: Resolving first applies to implicit frustration, not an explicit human request; delaying here ignores a direct instruction.
distractors.D: Interrogating the customer about why they want a human adds friction and ignores the explicit request.

stem: An order lookup tool returns 40+ fields per order, but only 5 are relevant to return processing. Across a long conversation these full payloads accumulate and crowd out context. What is the recommended approach?

options.A: Switch to a model with a larger context window
options.B: Ask the customer to read the order details aloud to avoid the tool call
options.C: Trim the tool output to only the relevant fields before it enters the conversation context
options.D: Raise max_tokens so the extra fields fit

explanation: Verbose tool outputs cause context bloat. Trimming to only the fields the task needs, before they accumulate, is the core mitigation and keeps the context lean across many turns.

distractors.A: A bigger window delays the problem and adds cost; the waste of 35 irrelevant fields per call remains.
distractors.B: Routing data through the customer is error-prone and unnecessary when the tool already returns it.
distractors.D: max_tokens governs output length, not the size of accumulated tool results in context.

stem: During a long codebase-exploration session, Claude Code starts giving inconsistent answers and refers to "typical patterns" rather than the specific classes it identified earlier. The verbose discovery output has filled the context. What is the most appropriate first action?

options.A: Restart from scratch in a brand-new session
options.B: Ask the same questions repeatedly until answers stabilize
options.C: Run /compact to condense the filled context while continuing the same session
options.D: Raise the temperature so the model is more creative

explanation: When context fills with verbose discovery output and answers degrade, /compact condenses the conversation so the session can continue with the important information retained. It directly targets the bloat causing degradation.

distractors.A: A full restart throws away all the discovered findings and wastes the prior exploration.
distractors.B: Re-asking does not reclaim context space and will keep producing degraded, generic answers.
distractors.D: Higher temperature increases variability, worsening the inconsistency.

stem: A customer provides only a common name, "John Smith." The get_customer tool returns three distinct accounts matching that name. What should the agent do before taking any account action?

options.A: Ask the customer for an additional identifier (email, order number, or account ID) to disambiguate
options.B: Pick the account with the most recent activity as the most likely match
options.C: Proceed with all three accounts in parallel and let the customer correct any mistakes
options.D: Pick the account whose city best matches the customer's IP geolocation

explanation: When a lookup returns multiple customer matches, the agent should request additional identifiers to disambiguate rather than guessing. Heuristic selection risks acting on the wrong person's account.

distractors.B: "Most recent activity" is a heuristic that can easily select the wrong customer.
distractors.C: Acting on all three accounts risks exposing or modifying other customers' data.
distractors.D: IP geolocation is an unreliable heuristic and can act on the wrong account.

stem: A document-analysis subagent fails on 2 of 5 documents (one timeout, one parse error) but succeeds on 3. Its current behavior is to return an empty result set marked as successful so the workflow does not halt. Why is this the wrong design?

options.A: It should terminate the entire workflow so a human can intervene
options.B: It should retry the failed documents forever until they succeed
options.C: It is correct — returning empty-as-success keeps the pipeline running
options.D: It silently suppresses errors and discards partial results, hiding the failures from the coordinator and corrupting downstream synthesis

explanation: Returning empty results marked as success is the silent-suppression anti-pattern: the coordinator cannot tell that 2 documents failed or that 3 succeeded, so synthesis silently treats incomplete data as complete. The subagent should return structured error context plus the partial (3 successful) results.

distractors.A: Terminating the whole workflow on a partial failure discards the 3 successful results and over-reacts.
distractors.B: Unbounded retries can hang the pipeline; retries must be bounded and failures eventually reported with context.
distractors.C: Empty-as-success hides failures and makes the system look complete when it is not.

stem: A web-search subagent hits a transient timeout, then propagates {"status": "error", "message": "search unavailable"} to the coordinator. The coordinator blindly retries the same query, which times out again. What two problems does this reveal?

options.A: The subagent should average across whatever data it has and report success
options.B: The coordinator should never retry, and the subagent should escalate to a human
options.C: The error should have been a JSON-RPC protocol error, and the coordinator should ignore it
options.D: The subagent should attempt local recovery for transient failures, and when it does propagate, it must include structured context (failure type, attempted query, partial results, alternatives)

explanation: Two issues: (1) transient failures should first be handled by local recovery in the subagent, and (2) any propagated error must carry structured context — failure type, what was attempted, partial results, and potential alternatives — so the coordinator can decide intelligently rather than blindly retrying.

distractors.A: Fabricating success from incomplete data is exactly the silent-suppression anti-pattern.
distractors.B: Retries are appropriate for transient faults with the right context; jumping straight to human escalation is premature.
distractors.C: Tool/agent failures should be reported as readable error context the coordinator can reason over, not buried in protocol errors.

stem: A coordinator must distinguish between a search tool that timed out and a search tool that completed successfully but found zero matching records. Why does this distinction matter for reliability?

options.A: An access failure (timeout/service down) needs a retry decision, while a valid empty result is correct data and must not trigger retries or be reported as an error
options.B: Both should be reported as empty successes to keep the pipeline simple
options.C: Only the empty result needs handling; timeouts can be ignored
options.D: It does not matter; both should be treated as failures and retried

explanation: An access failure (timeout, service down) is unresolved and may warrant a retry; a valid empty result is a successful query that genuinely found nothing. Conflating them either wastes retries on real zero-result answers or silently drops real failures, so error reporting must keep them distinguishable.

distractors.B: Marking a timeout as an empty success hides a real failure from the coordinator.
distractors.C: Ignoring timeouts means real access failures silently corrupt the results.
distractors.D: Retrying a genuine zero-result query wastes resources and can never "succeed" differently.

stem: A final research report states "Battery storage costs fell 76% over the last decade" with no source. The synthesis step compressed findings and dropped the source URLs, excerpts, and dates from the subagents. What design change best prevents this loss of provenance?

options.A: Have the synthesis agent re-search each claim to re-attach a source after the fact
options.B: Append a generic bibliography of every URL visited at the end of the report
options.C: Require subagents to output structured claim-source mappings (source URL/document, relevant excerpt, publication or collection date) that are preserved through synthesis
options.D: Instruct the synthesis agent to only include claims it is confident about

explanation: Provenance is lost when findings are compressed without preserving claim-source links. Requiring subagents to emit structured claim-source mappings — URL/document, excerpt, and date — keeps each claim traceable through summarization rather than relying on after-the-fact reconstruction.

distractors.A: Re-searching after the fact is unreliable and may attach a different source than the one that produced the claim.
distractors.B: A bulk bibliography does not map specific claims to specific sources, so individual statistics remain unverifiable.
distractors.D: Confidence filtering does not restore the missing source attribution for the claims that remain.

stem: Two sources report a key figure differently: one says "45% growth (2022 data)" and another says "52% growth (2024 data)." The synthesis agent must decide how to present this. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Silently keep the higher figure since it is more recent
options.B: Report both figures with their source attribution and collection dates, annotating the conflict so the coordinator/reader can reconcile
options.C: Drop both figures because they conflict and report only that growth occurred
options.D: Average the two figures and report 48.5% as a single number

explanation: Conflicting source values should be annotated with their source attribution and dates rather than arbitrarily resolved. Preserving the dates also prevents a temporal difference from being misread as a contradiction. The reconciliation decision belongs to the coordinator/reader, not a silent heuristic.

distractors.A: Silently picking one value discards provenance and hides a real conflict.
distractors.C: Dropping both throws away genuine, datable data points.
distractors.D: Averaging fabricates a number neither source reported and erases the temporal distinction.

stem: You explored a large codebase yesterday and built up significant findings in the session. Overnight, three specific files were modified by teammates. You want to continue the investigation today. What is the best approach?

options.A: Start a fresh session and re-explore the entire codebase from scratch
options.B: Resume with --resume and explicitly tell the agent which three files changed so it can re-analyze them in a targeted way
options.C: Resume with --resume and let the agent rediscover any changes naturally as it works
options.D: Start a fresh session because resuming will always operate on stale data

explanation: When prior context is mostly still valid but a few files changed, resume with --resume AND explicitly inform the agent which files changed so it can re-analyze them. This preserves the valid prior work while preventing the agent from reasoning over stale tool results for the changed files.

distractors.A: Re-exploring everything discards a large amount of still-valid prior work.
distractors.C: Without being told, the agent may keep using stale cached tool results for the changed files and never notice.
distractors.D: Resuming is appropriate when most context is valid; a fresh start is only needed when prior results are extensively stale.

stem: During a long, multi-phase codebase exploration you are worried key findings from early phases will be lost as the session grows. Which combination best preserves them across context boundaries?

options.A: Persist key findings to scratchpad files, delegate focused questions to subagents, and inject phase summaries into the initial context of the next phase
options.B: Increase the context window and disable any compaction
options.C: Keep everything in the conversation and rely on the model's memory of earlier turns
options.D: Restart the session between phases to keep each phase's context clean

explanation: The recommended pattern is to externalize findings into scratchpad files, delegate narrow questions to subagents while the main agent coordinates, and summarize each phase's key findings before starting the next phase (injecting those summaries into the new context). This keeps durable knowledge available across context boundaries.

distractors.B: A larger window still degrades and fills; without externalized findings, early phases are still lost.
distractors.C: Relying on in-context memory is exactly what fails in long sessions as context degrades.
distractors.D: Restarting between phases loses cross-phase continuity unless findings are carried forward via summaries/scratchpads.

stem: A team wants the agent to auto-escalate cases it is unsure about. A proposal is to have the model output a self-reported confidence score per case and escalate when it falls below a threshold. Why is this routing signal unreliable?

options.A: Confidence scores always trend high, so the threshold can simply be raised
options.B: Confidence scores are only valid for extraction tasks, not support routing
options.C: Confidence scores are too expensive to compute at scale
options.D: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated — models are often confidently wrong on exactly the hard cases that should escalate

explanation: Self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated: a model can be highly confident yet wrong, particularly on difficult cases — the very ones that need escalation. Routing on this signal lets hard, confidently-wrong cases slip through. A more reliable approach uses an independent review/verification instance rather than the model grading itself.

distractors.A: The failure is not a simple offset that a higher threshold fixes; confidence does not track correctness.
distractors.B: Poor calibration of self-reported confidence is a general property, not specific to extraction.
distractors.C: Cost is not the core issue; calibration is.

stem: An extraction pipeline reports 97% overall accuracy on a labeled set, so the team proposes turning off human review entirely. What is the strongest objection?

options.A: Accuracy should be measured at higher temperature first
options.B: The model should self-report confidence and route low-confidence items, replacing all validation
options.C: 97% is fine; human review can be removed immediately
options.D: Aggregate accuracy can mask segment-level failures — specific document types or fields may perform far worse — so validate accuracy stratified by document type and field before reducing review

explanation: A high aggregate metric can hide poor performance on particular document types or fields. Before reducing human review you must validate stratified by document type AND field to confirm performance is acceptable across all segments, not just on average.

distractors.A: Temperature is irrelevant to whether the aggregate masks segment-level failures.
distractors.B: Self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated and cannot, by itself, substitute for stratified validation.
distractors.C: Acting on the aggregate alone is exactly the trap; it can hide a failing segment.

stem: An extraction call fails a semantic check: the line items do not sum to the stated invoice total (the JSON is syntactically valid). You implement retry-with-error-feedback that resends the document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation error. What additional safeguard is most important?

options.A: Bound the number of retries and, when the bound is exhausted, route the item to human review with the captured error context
options.B: Switch to a larger model only after 50 consecutive failures
options.C: Allow unlimited retries until the extraction eventually validates
options.D: Disable validation so the extraction always succeeds

explanation: Retry-with-error-feedback must be bounded. Some documents will never validate (e.g., the source itself is inconsistent), so unbounded retries waste cost and can loop forever. After the retry bound is hit, escalate the item to human review carrying the validation error context.

distractors.B: An arbitrary 50-failure threshold is still effectively unbounded per item and delays human handling.
distractors.C: Unlimited retries can loop indefinitely on inputs that can never satisfy the check, burning cost.
distractors.D: Disabling validation hides genuine extraction errors — the opposite of reliability.

stem: A research synthesis must produce a report when one subagent's source domain was unreachable, so that subtopic has thin coverage while others are well-supported. The coordinator's instinct is to abort the whole report. What is the most reliable behavior?

options.A: Continue with the partial results, explicitly annotate which findings are well-supported versus which subtopics have gaps due to unavailable sources, and proceed
options.B: Silently fill the gap with the model's general knowledge so the report looks complete
options.C: Abort the report entirely because one subtopic is incomplete
options.D: Replace the missing subtopic by duplicating findings from a different subtopic

explanation: Graceful degradation: a single source/subagent failure should not terminate the whole workflow. Continue with partial results and annotate coverage — distinguishing well-established findings from subtopics with gaps caused by unavailable sources — so the report is honest and still useful.

distractors.B: Backfilling with general knowledge fabricates coverage and destroys provenance.
distractors.C: Terminating on a single failure discards substantial valid work and is the anti-pattern to avoid.
distractors.D: Copying unrelated findings misrepresents the missing subtopic.

stem: Downstream agents in your pipeline have small context budgets. A research subagent currently returns long prose with its full reasoning chain, overflowing those budgets. How should the subagent's output be redesigned, and how should the final report render mixed content?

options.A: Return raw tool outputs unchanged and let each downstream agent trim them itself
options.B: Return the full prose and reasoning verbatim, and convert the entire final report to a single uniform format
options.C: Return structured outputs (key facts, citations, relevance scores) with required metadata, and render content types appropriately — financial data as tables, news as prose, technical findings as structured lists
options.D: Return only a one-sentence summary with no metadata, and render everything as a flat bullet list

explanation: When downstream agents have limited context budgets, subagents should return compact structured data — key facts, citations, relevance scores, and required metadata (dates, source locations) — instead of verbose prose and reasoning chains. The final report should also render each content type in its natural form (tables for financial data, prose for news, structured lists for technical findings) rather than forcing one uniform format.

distractors.A: Pushing trimming downstream wastes each agent's scarce budget receiving the bloat first.
distractors.B: Verbatim prose overflows small budgets, and a single uniform format misrepresents different content types.
distractors.D: Stripping all metadata destroys provenance, and a flat bullet list is wrong for tabular financial data.

stem: Your support agent resolves only 55% of issues against a target of 80%. Analysis shows miscalibration in both directions: it escalates simple cases a human should not need to see, yet attempts to handle complex policy exceptions autonomously. How do you improve its escalation calibration?

options.A: Add explicit escalation criteria to the prompt, with few-shot examples showing which cases to escalate and which to handle autonomously
options.B: Have the model self-rate its confidence (1-10) on each case and escalate automatically below a threshold
options.C: Train a separate classifier on historical ticket data to make the escalation decision
options.D: Run sentiment analysis on customer messages and escalate when negative sentiment is detected

explanation: The root cause of two-directional miscalibration is unclear decision boundaries: the agent does not know what it should and should not handle. Explicit escalation criteria paired with few-shot examples teach the boundary directly, fixing both over-escalation of simple cases and over-confidence on complex policy exceptions.

distractors.B: LLM self-rated confidence is poorly calibrated — the model can be confidently wrong on exactly the hard cases that should escalate, so it is an unreliable routing signal.
distractors.C: A separately trained classifier is overengineering when the problem is simply that the decision boundary has never been clearly communicated to the agent.
distractors.D: Sentiment measures the customer's mood, not case complexity; an angry customer can have a trivial issue and a calm one a complex policy exception.

stem: Your document-analysis subagent encounters a corrupted PDF file that it cannot parse. When designing the system's error handling, what is the most effective way to handle this failure?

options.A: Return an error with context to the coordinator agent, allowing it to decide how to proceed
options.B: Silently skip the corrupted document and continue processing the remaining files to avoid interrupting the workflow
options.C: Automatically retry parsing the document three times with exponential backoff before reporting a failure
options.D: Throw an exception that terminates the entire research workflow

explanation: Returning an error with context to the coordinator lets it make an informed decision — skip the file, try an alternative parsing method, or notify the user — while maintaining full visibility into the failure.

distractors.B: Silently skipping hides the failure from the coordinator, so the final output appears complete when a source document was never analyzed.
distractors.C: File corruption is deterministic, not transient; retrying with backoff wastes time on a failure that will never resolve itself.
distractors.D: One unparseable file should not kill the entire workflow and discard all other valid progress.

stem: After calling `get_customer` and `lookup_order`, your support agent has all available system data but still faces uncertainty. Which situation is the most justified trigger for calling `escalate_to_human`?

options.A: A customer wants to cancel an order that shipped yesterday and arrives tomorrow; the agent should escalate because the customer might change their mind after receiving the package.
options.B: A customer claims they never received an order, but tracking shows it was delivered and signed for at their address three days ago; the agent should escalate because presenting contradictory evidence could harm the customer relationship.
options.C: A customer requests competitor price matching; your policies allow price adjustments for price drops on your own site within 14 days but say nothing about competitor prices, so the agent should escalate for policy interpretation.
options.D: A customer message contains both a billing question and a product return; the agent should escalate so a human can coordinate both issues in one interaction.

explanation: Competitor price matching is a genuine policy gap: the documented rules cover price drops on the company's own site but are silent on competitor prices. An agent must never invent or extend policy on its own — interpreting or extending the rules is exactly the kind of judgment that requires human escalation.

distractors.A: Cancelling a shipped order is a standard operational case the agent can handle under existing policy; speculating about whether the customer might later change their mind is not an escalation criterion.
distractors.B: Tracking data directly contradicts the claim, and presenting documented evidence is within the agent's capability; discomfort with a conversation is not a policy gap requiring human judgment.
distractors.D: Handling multiple issues in one interaction is exactly what a capable agent should do via decomposition; a multi-part request alone does not justify escalation.

stem: Over several turns discussing investment strategy, a user stated "I have a very low risk tolerance" and later "I want to maximize my returns." They now ask: "What should I invest in?" Which approach best ensures the recommendation aligns with the user's actual priority?

options.A: Surface the contradiction between the two stated preferences and ask the user to clarify which matters more.
options.B: Provide two separate recommendations, one for each stated preference.
options.C: Proceed using the most recently stated preference as the governing one.
options.D: Recommend a balanced portfolio without addressing the conflict.

explanation: Low risk tolerance and maximizing returns are fundamentally incompatible goals. When a user's stated preferences directly contradict each other, surfacing the conflict and asking for clarification is the only approach guaranteed to align with their true intent — every alternative substitutes an assumption that may be wrong.

distractors.B: Two parallel recommendations dodge the decision and push the unresolved contradiction back onto the user without even explaining that the goals conflict.
distractors.C: Recency is an arbitrary tiebreaker — the user never retracted the earlier risk constraint, so the latest statement is not evidence of their actual priority.
distractors.D: A balanced portfolio silently substitutes the assistant's compromise for the user's unknown priority and hides the fact that a conflict exists at all.

stem: In your playlist assistant, users refine their preferences over multiple conversation turns. Two messages after a user said "I love jazz," Claude asks "What genres do you enjoy?" What is the most likely cause?

options.A: Claude requires a vector database connection to maintain conversation memory, and none is configured.
options.B: The model's context window has been exceeded, so earlier turns were dropped.
options.C: The Claude API requires a `session_id` parameter to link requests, which your application is not sending.
options.D: Your application is not including the prior conversation turns in the `messages` array of each API request.

explanation: The Claude API is stateless — it has no server-side memory of previous calls. Every request must carry the full conversation history in the `messages` array; if the application sends only the latest message, Claude has no knowledge of anything said earlier, including "I love jazz."

distractors.A: Claude has no vector-database dependency; conversation memory is simply whatever the caller includes in the request.
distractors.B: A two-message exchange is nowhere near any context-window limit, so truncation cannot explain the loss.
distractors.C: The Messages API has no `session_id` parameter; maintaining conversation state is entirely the application's responsibility.

stem: After a 40-minute cooking-assistant session, the conversation reaches 78,000 tokens. The history includes the user's allergies, recipe scaling calculations, clarified cooking terms, and general discussion. You must reduce token usage while preserving important information. Which approach best balances preservation with token reduction?

options.A: Summarize the entire conversation history into a single condensed narrative.
options.B: Keep only the most recent 20,000 tokens and drop everything earlier.
options.C: Extract critical structured data (allergies, quantities, preferences) into a compact block, summarize the general discussion, and keep recent exchanges verbatim.
options.D: Store the full conversation externally and retrieve relevant parts via semantic search on each turn.

explanation: The hybrid approach preserves the highest-value information at the lowest token cost: safety-critical precise facts like allergies and quantities are extracted into a compact structured block (immune to summarization's precision loss), low-value general discussion is compressed, and recent exchanges stay verbatim for conversational coherence.

distractors.A: Whole-history summarization compresses safety-critical precise facts like allergies into lossy prose — exactly the data that must survive exactly.
distractors.B: A pure recency window silently discards the allergy and recipe-scaling information stated early in the session.
distractors.D: External storage with per-turn semantic retrieval is architectural overkill for a single session, and retrieval can miss critical facts that are not semantically similar to the current query.

stem: Users of your conversational assistant report that latency increases and costs rise as conversations exceed 50 turns. What is the primary cause?

options.A: The entire conversation history is sent with each API request, so input tokens grow with every turn.
options.B: The model generates progressively longer responses as conversations continue.
options.C: Database operations slow down as the stored conversation history grows.
options.D: The model builds an internal user profile that requires more processing as it grows.

explanation: Claude's API is stateless — each request must include the complete conversation history in the `messages` array. As the conversation grows, every request carries more input tokens, which directly increases both processing latency and per-request cost.

distractors.B: Response length is governed by the request and content, not by conversation age; output growth is not inherent to long conversations.
distractors.C: Database latency in your application is independent of model API latency and would not explain rising API token costs.
distractors.D: The API is stateless; the model maintains no internal profile or state between calls.

stem: Your book-club assistant has accumulated 85,000 tokens of conversation history over three months of weekly sessions. When a user asks "What did we conclude about the theme of isolation?", the assistant gives generic answers instead of referencing the previous discussions. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Apply rolling-window truncation so only the most recent sessions stay in context.
options.B: Use progressive summarization that captures the key conclusions from each session.
options.C: Index the conversation history with semantic embeddings and retrieve the relevant past exchanges when a question references earlier discussion.
options.D: Add structured XML tags marking discussion conclusions throughout the history.

explanation: Semantic retrieval over the archived history is the only approach that scales to months of discussion while still surfacing the specific past exchanges a question refers to. The relevant conclusion about "isolation" can be found and injected on demand, preserving the specificity that generic answers lack.

distractors.A: Truncation discards exactly the months-old discussions the user is asking about.
distractors.B: Progressive summarization compresses specific conclusions into abstractions — the generic-answer problem would persist in a different form.
distractors.D: Tagging conclusions requires restructuring all past content and still does not solve how to fit or locate 85,000 tokens of history at question time.

stem: During QA testing, Claude follows your system-prompt guidelines for the first 10-15 turns of a conversation, but later responses begin to deviate. The conversation is still well within token limits. What is the best solution?

options.A: Move the behavioral guidelines out of the system prompt and into the first user message.
options.B: Automatically start a new conversation after 20 turns.
options.C: Insert user-role messages that reinforce the guidelines at natural conversation breakpoints.
options.D: Use post-response validation that detects non-compliant responses and regenerates them.

explanation: Periodic reinforcement directly counters instruction drift: as conversation history accumulates and dilutes the system prompt's influence, re-injecting the guidelines at natural breakpoints re-establishes the constraints while preserving the conversation. It is preventive, cheap, and keeps context intact.

distractors.A: A first user message carries less persistent authority than the system prompt and drifts into the middle of the context just as quickly.
distractors.B: Resetting the conversation destroys accumulated context and degrades the user experience to fix a problem that in-place reminders solve.
distractors.D: Regeneration is corrective rather than preventive, adds latency and cost on every violation, and itself requires a reliable compliance detector.

stem: Users of your event-planning assistant frequently send requests like "Book a venue for the party." The assistant responds with four or more clarifying questions, and 35% of users abandon the conversation. What approach best improves the clarification-versus-friction trade-off?

options.A: Proceed using hidden default values for the unspecified details.
options.B: Ask all the clarifying questions at once in a single compound message.
options.C: State reasonable assumptions explicitly, proceed with them, and invite the user to correct any that are wrong.
options.D: Replace the conversation with a structured intake form that collects all details up front.

explanation: Stating assumptions explicitly and proceeding gives the user an immediately useful response — removing the friction that drives abandonment — while keeping them informed and in control: any wrong assumption is visible and trivially correctable.

distractors.A: Hidden defaults produce results the user cannot sanity-check — they never learn what was assumed, so errors surface late and erode trust.
distractors.B: A wall of questions still demands the full upfront effort that is driving abandonment, just compressed into one message.
distractors.D: A form adds even more upfront friction than the questions did, working directly against the abandonment problem.

stem: Your assistant uses a contractor-persona system prompt. Early turns follow the persona rules, but by turn 7 the assistant is giving generic advice. The whole conversation is only about 2,500 tokens. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: System prompts only establish initial behavior and stop applying after the first few turns.
options.B: The model's attention mechanism weakens over long contexts as turns accumulate.
options.C: Accumulated assistant responses in the history increasingly dilute the system prompt's influence, as the model pattern-matches to its own prior outputs.
options.D: The system prompt is only sent on the first API call and is absent from later requests.

explanation: As assistant responses accumulate, the proportion of context reflecting the system prompt's constraints shrinks relative to the model's own generated content. The model increasingly pattern-matches to its prior outputs — if those drifted slightly generic, the drift compounds — which explains persona decay even at a short 2,500 tokens.

distractors.A: System prompts apply for the whole conversation on every request; there is no built-in expiry after early turns.
distractors.B: Attention-related degradation is a long-context phenomenon; it does not explain drift at only 2,500 tokens.
distractors.D: In a correctly built integration the system prompt is included in every API call, and a genuinely missing prompt would cause an abrupt behavior change, not gradual drift.
